item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from Wikimedia Commons under Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember reading this a long time ago and thinking it was subpar. Rereading, I really liked the prose of the D-Class testimony, and the rest of the article is intriguing / holds up. +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I like it. I never thought of connecting this with Prometheus but I can see them snatching up old Edison labs prototypes when they become available.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Considering the nature of Edison era scientists, its very likely Prometheus Labs, or a similar anomalous corporation may stumble upon the research presented here and try and work it into something that won't liquefy people's brains. I could see PL using the tech here to eventually create SCP-2167. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Mr. Edison appears to have associated himself with the organization for a period of only two months following which he left, denying any involvement. During this two-month period several prototypes were created. SCP-1576 appears to be the only one created with any functionality. As of 1/12/1950, SCP-1576 has been contained and classified as Euclid.

To this:

Mr. Edison appears to have associated himself with the organization for a period of only two months following which he left, denying any involvement. During this two-month period several prototypes were created. SCP-1576 appears to be the only one created with any functionality, though recovered evidence indicates agents for Prometheus Labs acquiring and reverse engineering nonfunctional prototypes to form SCP-2167 and similar anomalies. As of 1/12/1950, SCP-1576 has been contained and classified as Euclid.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tiny people in the head? This just feels so utterly dumb. The effect is kind of interesting, but, really tiny people in the head is a terrible awful bad wrong perplexing idea. I would hone out the "Edison had a distorted perception of reality or didn't know what he was doing" angle.

Tiny people in the head

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"and expelled" needs a verb in there, maybe 'be'.

This is a weird little skip. I like the historical context, and I especially like objects that function out of misbelief made real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just downright bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, the article just felt like it didn't have strong enough of a hook for me. That aside, I'm mainly commenting to point out a few grammatical issues I noticed, which I found a bit jarring when I read through the article.

No personnel are to be admitted access to this chamber.

I… I think this might just be me, but is "admitted access" a real phrase? I feel like it should either be "admitted" or "permitted access" here.

In the event that an instance is located, the cover story of CDC intervention for a new form of meningitis.

This is a sentence fragment. There needs to be some sort of verb in that second clause.

All that aside, I also noticed that you use the word "instance" a lot in the article, including at one point four times in the same short paragraph. It isn't really something that needs to be fixed, but it stood out when I was reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The documentation is impressive, along with the Edison stuff, but the SCP is pretty meh. The effect is simple, maybe too simple. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get a very fifthist (proto-fifthist?) vibe, but that might just be me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This is super good. It's relevant historically, you imply at greater things going on, and overall tell a whole story in just a single SCP document. Nice work, +1 heartily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1.- Why do we keep them locked until they die, instead of incinerating them right away? Seems pointless.
2.- Has there been any test to check the stuff they're 'expelling' and why is it contagious? Are they infected neurones somehow, that mystically manage to go up to the new host's brain or what?
3.- Had the apparaus been checked to see if it produces the infection physically by sending a virus-equivalent, or is a sound the cause, or any other reason?

Until that's fixed, I go fo -1, no hook and not interesting imo~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glitch seems to have been fixed, or it just worked itself out. My check on the ratings showed no double vote. That said, no vote, the concept just didn't grab me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only complaint is that the placement of the testimony and documents make the flow seem a bit weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still don't understand why we don't just kill the infected right away1 if all we're going to do is put them in a room until they die.

+1 regardless

Footnotes
1. With the exception of a few D-Class for testing purposes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No SCP article before this one has ever caused me to sneeze when I was done reading it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wikidot glitch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never heard of that happening before, but it happens to be showing a double downvote on my screen too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, plus one and all that.

Side note, I looked at who voted what for this page out of curiosity, and one member is listed twice as voting -, and doing the math shows that they are both counting against the page. Just thought it was something that should be known.

If this is just something that happens and I'm dumb for not knowing about it, I apologize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: stay out of my territory

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: edison is so alone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but the first thing I thought when I saw that picture was "edison hate future". Upvoted anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea of tiny people living in our heads that from consciousness and that you can talk to dead people by finding and bringing together all the tiny people that lived in that person.

However, if they are real, you didn't do enough with them, and if they are just Edison's erroneous view of reality (which I think was your intention here), then the object just isn't interesting enough.

I want to read more about tiny people. Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really find it interesting at all.
